---
title: "VS Code & Talapas"
author: "Shannon N. Snyder"
date: "2026-04-20"
---

# Visual Studio Code --- Setup Guide for the Single Cell Workshop

**What is VS Code?** Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free, cross-platform editor from Microsoft that lets you browse file systems and edit text documents, including on remote servers such as Talapas. It has a built-in terminal that lets you access the command line, making it convenient for running scripts during the workshop.

## 1. Installation

Download and install VS Code from the official site:

<https://code.visualstudio.com/download>

Once installed, open VS Code. If a permissions pop-up appears, allow the requested access. From the main interface you can browse and open folders on your local computer.

## 2. Connecting to Remote HPC (Talapas)

VS Code can connect directly to the University of Oregon's Talapas HPC cluster via SSH. Follow the steps below.

### Step 1 --- Install Remote Extensions

In the left sidebar, click the **Extensions** tab and search for `ssh`. Install the following extensions:

-   **Remote --- SSH**
-   **Remote Explorer**
-   **Remote --- SSH: Editing Configuration Files** (optional but recommended)

### Step 2 --- Add the Talapas SSH Host

Open **Remote Explorer** from the left sidebar. Click the **+** icon next to **SSH** and enter the following command, replacing `[yourduckid]` with your UO Duck ID:

``` bash
ssh [yourduckid]@login.talapas.uoregon.edu
```

Press **Enter**, then press **Enter** again to save to the default config file.

### Step 3 --- Connect to Talapas

In the **Remote Explorer** dropdown, expand the **SSH** section. You should see `login.talapas.uoregon.edu`. Click the **→** arrow to start the connection, then:

1.  Enter your Duck ID password when prompted (characters will not appear as you type).
2.  When asked for Duo confirmation, type `1` and press **Enter** to receive a Duo push.
3.  Once connected, a blue banner reading **SSH: login.talapas.uoregon.edu** will appear in the bottom-left corner.

Click **Open Folder** to browse directories on Talapas. It is helpful to open the base directory of your current project. You may also want a second window pointed at your home directory:

```
/home/[yourduckid]/nereus
```

### Helpful Tips

-   Use the integrated terminal (**Terminal → New Terminal**) to run scripts directly on Talapas without leaving VS Code.
-   You can have multiple VS Code windows open --- one for each server or project.
-   The file explorer on the left updates in real time, making it easy to navigate complex directory structures.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# About Talapas --- UO's HPC Cluster

This section extends the guide above with additional context drawn from the University of Oregon's [Research Advanced Computing Services (RACS)](https://racs.uoregon.edu/) and the [Talapas Knowledge Base](https://uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base/). It is here so you have the essentials for working on Talapas in one place.

## What is Talapas?

**Talapas** is UO's flagship high-performance computing (HPC) cluster, managed by Research Advanced Computing Services (RACS). The name comes from the Chinook word for *coyote*, the educator and keeper of knowledge. It combines centrally funded open-use resources with researcher-funded ("condo") resources and, at last count, provides over **14,500 CPU cores, 129 TB of memory, and roughly 89 GPUs** (AMD Milan, Intel Broadwell/Cascadelake/Icelake/Sapphire Rapids CPUs; NVIDIA A40 through H100 GPUs; memory configurations from 128 GB to 6 TB per node).

## Getting an Account

To work on Talapas you need a UO Duck ID and a Talapas account tied to a research group (called a **PIRG** --- Principal Investigator Research Group). If you don't yet have an account, request one at:

<https://racs.uoregon.edu/request-access>

Your Talapas username is your Duck ID (the part of your UO email before `@uoregon.edu`). Your password is the same University-wide credential and can be managed via UO's password reset page.

## Logging In

The Talapas login host is `login.talapas.uoregon.edu`. Connect with SSH:

``` bash
ssh [yourduckid]@login.talapas.uoregon.edu
```

-   For security, **no characters are displayed while you type your password**.
-   SSH logins use **Duo two-factor authentication**. When prompted, type `1` and press Enter to receive a Duo push, or follow the alternate options presented.
-   UO's VPN is recommended for off-campus logins; with the VPN you can also reach direct login nodes (`login1` ... `login4`) if needed.
-   Mac and Linux users can set up an **ED25519 SSH key** to avoid typing a password every session (Duo will still prompt when required by policy).

### Open OnDemand (browser-based access)

RACS also provides a web portal at <https://ondemand.talapas.uoregon.edu/> (Chrome or Firefox recommended) that lets you edit files, launch a shell, start Jupyter, or open an interactive X11 desktop (via VNC) without any SSH client. It is the easiest way to run RStudio or Jupyter sessions on Talapas.

## File Systems and Storage

Every Talapas user has access to several directories on the ~3 PB IBM Elastic Storage System (GPFS):

| Directory              | Size    | Purpose                                                                                                 |
|------------------------|---------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `/home/<duckid>/`      | 250 GB  | Personal files, scripts, dotfiles. Small software installs.                                             |
| `/projects/<PIRG>/`    | 2 TB    | Shared project data for your research group.                                                            |
| `/scratch/<PIRG>/`     | 20 TB   | High-throughput working space. **Files not accessed in the last 90 days are purged** on a rolling basis. |

::: {.callout-warning}
**You are responsible for backing up your data.** RACS does not back up Talapas file systems. Some snapshotting exists for home and project directories, but it is not a substitute for your own backups.
:::

File transfer options:

-   `scp` / `sftp` from the terminal.
-   [Filezilla](https://filezilla-project.org/) (cross-platform GUI for SCP/SFTP).
-   [**Globus**](https://www.globus.org/) --- RACS's recommended tool for large transfers, especially to/from the Large-Scale Research Storage (LSRS) system.

## Software Modules (Lmod)

Talapas uses [Lmod](https://lmod.readthedocs.io/) to manage centrally installed software. Key commands:

``` bash
module spider blast        # search for software by name
module avail               # list modules loadable without switching groups
module load samtools       # load a module (default version)
module load samtools/1.17  # load a specific version
module list                # show currently loaded modules
module unload samtools     # unload a module
module purge               # unload all modules
```

Modules are loaded per-shell --- you will need to `module load` again in every new session (or in every SLURM job script).

## Partitions and the SLURM Scheduler

Talapas uses the [SLURM](https://slurm.schedmd.com/) job scheduler. Jobs are submitted to **partitions** (queues), each with different limits and hardware:

| Partition         | Typical use                                   | Max wall time |
|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------|---------------|
| `compute`         | Standard CPU jobs                             | 1 day         |
| `computelong`     | Long CPU jobs                                 | 14 days       |
| `gpu` / `gpulong` | GPU jobs (short / long)                       | 1 day / 14 d  |
| `memory` / `memorylong` | High-memory CPU jobs (up to 4 TB RAM)   | 1 day / 14 d  |
| `interactive` / `interactivegpu` | Short interactive sessions     | 12 h / 8 h    |
| `preempt`         | Access to all nodes; jobs may be preempted    | Variable      |

All SLURM jobs **must specify an account** (your PIRG) for correct service charging. You can do this with `--account=<myPIRG>` on every command, or export it once in your `~/.bash_profile`:

``` bash
export SLURM_ACCOUNT=<myPIRG>
export SBATCH_ACCOUNT=<myPIRG>
```

See the separate SLURM tutorial for how to submit batch and interactive jobs. As a first rule: **do not run heavy computations on the login nodes.** Anything that takes more than a few seconds belongs in an interactive session (`srun --pty bash`) or a batch job (`sbatch my_script.sh`).

## Getting Help

-   [Talapas Knowledge Base](https://uoracs.github.io/talapas2-knowledge-base/) --- searchable docs and how-to articles.
-   [RACS website](https://racs.uoregon.edu/) --- overview of services.
-   [Talapas Service Desk](https://hpcrcf.atlassian.net/servicedesk/customer/portal/1) --- submit a ticket (include job IDs, scripts, and error messages).
-   **RACS Office Hours** --- in-person help at the Knight Campus 1st Floor tables (check the Knowledge Base homepage for the current term's schedule).
